learning_objectives: Define project management and its core terminology
Identify the role of the project manager
readings: Kerzner, Project Management: A Systems Approach, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Introduction to Project Management. This course provides the foundational knowledge needed for every subsequent PM course in the program.
key_concepts: Definition of a project: temporary endeavor with a unique deliverable
Triple constraint: scope, time, cost
Project vs. program vs. portfolio
The project life cycle: initiating, planning, executing, monitoring, closing
PMI and the PMBOK framework
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk-through of a real-world project charter for a campus building renovation
setup_requirements: Microsoft Project trial license or ProjectLibre installed

review.value: Scope, time, and cost are interdependent; changing one affects the others
Five process groups: Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing

class_schedule.topic: Project Life Cycle and Process Groups
learning_objectives: Describe the five process groups in detail
Map knowledge areas to process groups
Understand the iterative nature of project management
readings: Meredith & Mantel, Project Management: A Managerial Approach, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding the process groups is essential because every project, regardless of industry, follows this pattern.
key_concepts: Initiating: defining the project and obtaining authorization
Planning: establishing scope, objectives, and courses of action
Executing: coordinating people and resources to carry out the plan
Monitoring & Controlling: tracking and reviewing progress
Closing: formalizing acceptance and archiving lessons learned
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping a small software project through all five process groups using a timeline diagram

review.value: Planning and executing often overlap; monitoring feeds back into planning adjustments
Ten knowledge areas (scope, schedule, cost, quality, resource, communication, risk, procurement, stakeholder, integration) intersect with process groups

learning_objectives: Compare functional, matrix, and projectized organizational structures
Evaluate project selection methods including NPV and scoring models
Understand how organizational culture affects project success

lecture_notes.introduction: The organizational structure directly impacts how much authority a project manager has and how resources are allocated.
key_concepts: Functional organization: PM has little authority, resources owned by departments
Matrix organization: shared authority, weak/balanced/strong variants
Projectized organization: PM has full authority, dedicated teams
Project selection: benefit-cost ratio, NPV, IRR, scoring models
Organizational process assets vs. enterprise environmental factors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study comparing a matrix-structured tech company with a projectized construction firm

review.value: Balances functional expertise with project focus but creates dual-reporting complexity
Net Present Value discounts future cash flows to present; positive NPV projects add value

class_schedule.topic: The Project Charter and Stakeholder Identification
learning_objectives: Draft a project charter with all required components
Identify and classify stakeholders using a power-interest grid
Understand the role of the project sponsor

lecture_notes.introduction: The project charter is the document that formally authorizes the project and gives the PM authority to apply resources.
key_concepts: Charter components: purpose, objectives, scope summary, milestones, budget summary, PM authority
Stakeholder identification: brainstorming, expert judgment, document analysis
Power-interest grid: manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, monitor
Project sponsor role and responsibilities
Assumptions and constraints documentation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drafting a project charter for a university event management system using a template

review.value: Authorizes the project, names the PM, and provides high-level scope, budget, and timeline
Classifies stakeholders into four quadrants based on their power over and interest in the project

learning_objectives: Describe the components of a project management plan
Understand baseline concepts for scope, schedule, and cost

lecture_notes.introduction: Planning is where projects are won or lost. A solid plan provides the roadmap for execution and the baseline for monitoring.
key_concepts: Project management plan: integration of subsidiary plans
Work Breakdown Structure: hierarchical decomposition of deliverables
WBS dictionary: detailed description of each work package
Scope baseline = scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary
Rolling wave planning for uncertain future phases
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a WBS for a mobile app development project using sticky notes and digital tools
setup_requirements: WBS template in MS Project or WBS Chart Pro

review.value: Decomposes the total scope into manageable work packages; the 100% rule means the WBS captures all project work
Approved versions of scope, schedule, and cost used as reference points for measuring performance

learning_objectives: Describe key activities in the execution process group
Apply earned value management basics for monitoring
List the activities required to properly close a project

lecture_notes.introduction: Execution is where the plan becomes reality. Monitoring ensures the project stays on track, and closing ensures formal completion.
key_concepts: Directing and managing project work: deliverables, work performance data
Earned Value basics: PV, EV, AC, SPI, CPI
Change control: integrated change control process
Project closure: final deliverable acceptance, lessons learned, contract closure
Administrative closure vs. contract closure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating SPI and CPI from a sample project dataset and interpreting results

review.value: EV measures work performed in dollar terms; SPI = EV/PV (schedule), CPI = EV/AC (cost)
Formal acceptance of deliverables, release of resources, archival of documents, lessons learned session

quizzes.topic: Project Management Fundamentals and Process Groups

questions.question: A company is choosing between two projects. Project A has an NPV of $120,000 and a payback period of 3 years. Project B has an NPV of $95,000 and a payback period of 1.5 years. Which project should be selected based on NPV, and why?
questions.answer: Project A should be selected because NPV is the primary financial selection criterion, and $120,000 > $95,000. NPV accounts for the time value of money across the entire project life, while payback period only measures how quickly the investment is recovered and ignores cash flows beyond that point.

rubric.key: Explains NPV superiority over payback period

questions.question: Describe the key differences between a functional organization and a projectized organization in terms of project manager authority and resource allocation.
questions.answer: In a functional organization, the project manager has little to no formal authority, resources are owned by functional managers, and the PM must negotiate for resources. In a projectized organization, the PM has full authority over the project, team members are dedicated to the project full-time, and the PM controls the budget and schedule directly.

rubric.key: Correctly describes functional org PM authority

questions.question: What are the five essential components of a project charter, and why is the charter important before planning begins?
questions.answer: The five essential components are: (1) project purpose/justification, (2) measurable objectives, (3) high-level scope description, (4) summary budget and milestones, and (5) assigned project manager and authority level. The charter is important because it formally authorizes the project, gives the PM authority to apply organizational resources, and provides the high-level boundaries within which planning occurs.

rubric.key: Lists at least four correct components
Explains PM authority and resource connection

questions.question: Explain the 100% rule in WBS construction and provide an example of how violating it would cause problems.
questions.answer: The 100% rule states that the WBS must capture 100% of the work defined by the project scope, and each level must account for 100% of the work in its parent. Violating it means some work is undefined. For example, if a software project WBS includes 'Development' and 'Testing' but omits 'Deployment,' the deployment work has no budget, schedule, or resource allocation, leading to scope creep, cost overruns, and missed deadlines when deployment inevitably must happen.

rubric.key: Provides a concrete example of violation

major_assignments.title: Project Charter and Stakeholder Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a real or realistic project scenario and produce a complete project charter and stakeholder analysis. The project must have at least 10 identifiable stakeholders and a budget between $50,000 and $500,000.
objectives: Demonstrate ability to write a professional project charter
Apply stakeholder identification and classification techniques
Create a power-interest grid with management strategies
Articulate project justification using business case reasoning
deliverables: Stakeholder register with at least 10 stakeholders
Power-interest grid with management strategy for each quadrant
One-page executive summary suitable for sponsor presentation

criteria: All required charter components present and complete
Objectives are SMART (specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, time-bound)
Appropriate level of detail for initiating phase
At least 10 stakeholders identified with roles described
Management strategies are actionable and appropriate
Key stakeholder needs and expectations documented
Clear problem statement or opportunity description
Financial justification with basic cost-benefit analysis
Free of grammatical and typographical errors
Executive summary is concise and persuasive
Thorough stakeholder analysis with actionable strategies

weekly_quizzes.description: Biweekly quizzes assessing comprehension of project management fundamentals

assignments.description: Project charter, WBS, and planning deliverables demonstrating practical application

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering process groups, knowledge areas, and organizational structures

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all course material with scenario-based questions

class_participation.description: Active participation in discussions, case study analyses, and in-class exercises

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date without prior written approval.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. Study groups are encouraged for exam preparation but submitted work must be original.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all sources. Plagiarism or unauthorized collaboration will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to Microsoft Project (or ProjectLibre), a word processor, and a spreadsheet application. Campus lab computers are available.
